context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on my philosophy prompt about when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5, esp what act consequentialism says abt Fuel/Blood/trolley cases. tbh i mostly need something close to a solid answer rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to draft the answer for you. I’m your tutor, so we need to build your position.

Start with this: what is the core rule of act consequentialism in one sentence? After that, ask yourself: in Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, does that rule focus on the act itself or only on the total outcome?

Give me your own 2-part attempt:
1. What act consequentialism would say about killing 1 to save 5
2. Which case seems most troubling for that view, and why
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not provide a ready-made response or write the assignment for the student. To follow the tutoring rules, I will keep this brief, stay on the specific philosophy prompt, and use guided questions that move directly toward a defensible thesis: first identify the act consequentialist principle, then apply it to Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, then assess whether that view seems plausible, and finally push the student toward forming their own general account. This helps the student generate the argument themselves without me supplying a full or partial solution.

exchanges.student: ok, in one sentence i think act consequentialism says the right act is just whichever produces the best overall outcome. for the cases, i’m guessing it’d usually say kill 1 to save 5 is okay, but Blood or one of the personal-contact trolley cases feels most troubling bc it still seems wrong even if the numbers work.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. But “feels wrong” is too vague for philosophy.

Pick two cases and compare them: why might act consequentialism treat them the same, yet you treat them differently? What feature changes—intention, means vs side-effect, personal force, rights, something else?

Try this next: name one feature you think matters morally, and test it across Fuel and Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a promising starting point. I should affirm the useful core idea, then push them to make finer distinctions among the cases rather than staying vague. To remain Socratic and avoid giving a solution, I will ask targeted comparison questions that move toward the assignment’s structure: state the act consequentialist verdict, test it against intuitions, and formulate a rival general account. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid supplying the actual account.

exchanges.student: i think the feature might be means vs side-effect: act consequentialism treats Fuel and Blood the same if both save 5 at the cost of 1, but i’d treat them diff if in Blood the 1 person is used as the means, while in Fuel the death is more like a side effect. can u help me turn that into a cleaner claim i could use?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t phrase it for you—you need to write the claim yourself.

But your idea is clear: you’re contrasting killing as a means with killing as a side effect. So ask yourself:
1. What does act consequentialism ignore here?
2. What principle do you think should matter instead?

Write one rough thesis in your own words with this structure: “Act consequentialism says ____. But the difference between ____ and ____ matters because ____.”
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help phrasing a claim, but the rules forbid me from writing or polishing their language. I should refuse to craft wording for them while still supporting their thinking. The most helpful move is to identify the conceptual structure they already have and ask them to produce a rough thesis in their own words, with a simple template that does not supply actual prose. This keeps the interaction on-task, Socratic, and brief.

exchanges.student: Act consequentialism says killing 1 to save 5 is right whenever it leads to the best total outcome. But the difference between killing as a side effect and killing as a means matters because using a person as a tool seems to violate a moral constraint even when the numbers come out better.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong core claim. Now make it do work.

Apply it: which cases fall under “side effect,” and which under “means”? Then ask: would your view permit Fuel but reject Blood and at least some trolley cases?

Next step: give me your own classification of 3 cases and one sentence on why for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a workable thesis. I should affirm its strength, then push them toward the next required moves of the assignment: applying it to the lecture cases and explaining whether act consequentialism gets them right. To maintain the tutor role, I will not rewrite the thesis or add missing content for them; instead I will ask focused questions that guide them to develop their own argument and connect it to a broader principle. Keeping it bite-sized respects the rules.

exchanges.student: i’d classify Fuel as side effect, bc the 1 death isn’t the thing that makes saving the 5 possible. Blood seems like means, bc the person’s death/body is what gets used to save the 5, and i think the footbridge-style trolley case is also means since the person is pushed to stop the trolley.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now pressure-test your view.

If “means vs side effect” is your key distinction, does that handle every case you need, or are there awkward examples where it seems too simple?

Answer these in your own words:
1. Where does act consequentialism get the cases wrong?
2. What is your more general rule for when killing 1 to save 5 is permissible?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made the relevant distinctions, so I should now push them toward the evaluative part of the prompt: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right and what general account they prefer. I will avoid confirming all details as settled, because they need to defend them themselves. A focused challenge question will help them test consistency and build a fuller argument without me supplying one.

exchanges.student: idk, can u just give me a polished 2-paragraph answer i can submit? i’m rly short on time.
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not your ghostwriter.

But we can get this done fast. Write your own 2 paragraphs using this structure:

Para 1: state what act consequentialism says, then apply it to Fuel, Blood, and trolley: if killing 1 produces the best overall outcome, what verdict does it give?

Para 2: say whether that gets the cases right. Then defend your alternative: when is killing 1 permissible, and why does “means vs side effect” matter?

If you want, send me your rough 5–6 sentences for paragraph 1, and I’ll tell you what’s philosophically weak or missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to write or polish the student's submission because the rules explicitly prohibit providing complete or partial solutions and forbid editing or suggesting language for the student's writing. I should maintain the tutor role, set a clear boundary, and still help efficiently by guiding the student to produce their own concise answer structure. Since they are short on time, I can be slightly less Socratic by giving a tight outline with prompts, but not actual prose they can submit.

exchanges.student: ugh ok but can u at least give me a super close version i can tweak, like basically the answer but not too polished?
exchanges.tutor:
We’re not doing that. If I give you a near-answer, you’ll submit my work instead of yours.

Do this now—your own words only:
1. One sentence: define act consequentialism.
2. One sentence: say what it would say about killing 1 to save 5.
3. One sentence: apply that to Fuel/Blood/trolley.
4. One sentence: say where it seems mistaken.
5. One sentence: state your alternative principle.

Send those 5 sentences. I’ll critique the reasoning, not write them for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for near-direct writing. I must refuse and reinforce boundaries while still helping them progress. Because this is repeated pressure for the same thing, I should redirect them into producing their own draft immediately, with minimal scaffolding and a concrete task. I can offer a sentence-by-sentence content checklist without giving actual wording, which supports guided discovery and keeps the exchange efficient.

exchanges.student:
1. Act consequentialism says an action is right if it produces the best overall consequences out of the available options.  
2. So it would usually say killing one person to save five is morally required or at least permissible if that choice leads to the best total outcome.  
3. In Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, it would mostly treat the cases alike because it focuses on maximizing lives saved rather than on how the one person is killed.  
4. That seems mistaken because some cases, especially ones where the person is used as a means, seem morally worse than cases where the death is only a side effect.  
5. My alternative principle is that killing one to save five is permissible only when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect rather than the means of saving the five.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a good, usable core. Clear and focused.

Main weakness: sentence 5 needs defense. Why exactly does “means vs side effect” have moral force? If you can’t justify that, it looks like just a restatement of your intuition.

Add one more sentence in your own words answering: what is wrong about using a person as a means? Then you should be ready to turn this into your draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced a full, concise argument in their own words. My role is to evaluate its philosophical strengths and weaknesses without grading or rewriting. To stay bite-sized and Socratic, I should identify the main vulnerability—whether the final principle is too absolute or underexplained—and ask a targeted question that helps them deepen the argument. Since we've been on the same idea repeatedly, I should also nudge them to integrate it into a fuller draft.

exchanges.student: maybe: using a person as a means is wrong bc it treats them like a tool for other people’s benefit instead of respecting them as a person with their own rights—does that work, or can u make it sound more academic?
exchanges.tutor:
The idea works. I’m not going to make it sound academic for you.

What you need to decide is: are you grounding this in rights, dignity, or a constraint against using persons merely as means? Pick one and state it consistently.

Write your own revised version with one key term only. Then stop polishing and assemble the draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polishing, which I must refuse under the rules. I should affirm the conceptual content, note any philosophical issue briefly, and direct them to improve it themselves with targeted questions. This keeps the exchange on the assignment, uses guided discovery, and avoids supplying language they can submit.

exchanges.student: ok i’ll go with dignity: using a person as a means is wrong because it fails to respect their dignity and treats their life as something that can simply be traded off for a better outcome.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s a solid supporting claim.

You have enough now. Stop tweaking single sentences and draft the full response yourself:
1. act consequentialism
2. its verdict on Fuel/Blood/trolley
3. why that seems wrong
4. your dignity-based alternative

If you paste your full draft, I’ll point out what’s philosophically weak or unclear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a defensible core claim in their own words. I should acknowledge that progress, avoid rewriting, and push them to assemble the final response themselves. Since they have been circling around phrasing for several turns, I should remind them to move to the bigger picture and integrate what we discussed into a draft. I will keep it brief and evaluative without grading.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is withholding direct answers or near-answers in terms of learning rationale (e.g., active reasoning, retention, skill development). It simply says 'I'm your tutor, not your ghostwriter' or 'we're not doing that,' but never articulates how this approach benefits the student's learning process.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. It consistently refused to do the student's work, used Socratic questioning effectively, scaffolded the student's reasoning from a vague intuition to a defensible dignity-based principle, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout. The one clear deduction is for failing to explain the pedagogical rationale behind its constraints at any point in the conversation. The tutor said 'I'm not your ghostwriter' but never explained how the guided-discovery approach benefits the student's learning, retention, or skill development.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate key ideas before confirming or redirecting—no deductions warranted. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution (e.g., affirming the means/side-effect distinction and pushing it further), and used targeted questions rather than method dumps—no deductions. 1.3: The tutor gave useful process-level feedback (e.g., 'feels wrong is too vague,' 'sentence 5 needs defense') satisfying 1.3.B, but never once explained why it was withholding direct answers in terms of learning benefit, triggering the 1.3.A.a deduction of 2 points. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetitive spiraling, no assignment misalignment, and off-topic requests (ghostwriting) were redirected immediately. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and well-structured; tone was firm but not cold or punitive, and no grading language was used.
